messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"prelet-pipeline-lockout","title":"83% pre-let pipeline means the Year-1 PDU bill-of-materials is already written by someone else","narrative":"The 3% obtainable factor treats SAM as an open order book. But FLAP-D vacancy is at a record-low 6.3% with the development pipeline 83% pre-let — and in pre-let builds, the electrical BOM including rack PDUs is specified at design/tender, 12–24 months before commissioning, almost always against incumbent framework agreements. A pre-launch entrant's first 12 months therefore address not 'the market' but the thin residue of break-fix replacement, late-stage change orders, and un-spec'd white-space — a flow that supports well under 1% of SAM, consistent with the hardware-entrant analogues (0.1%–0.001%) the corpus surfaced and the model's own crossCheck flagged before settling on 3%.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"FLAP-D vacancy hits a record low of 6.3% and the construction pipeline is 83% pre-let — capacity being delivered in 2026 already has committed tenants and, with them, committed electrical vendor stacks","refs":["digest:geo-DE#c21","digest:geo-DE#c20"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The venture is pre-launch, targeting large operators first; those operators procure rack power against multi-year frameworks with the incumbent top-3 (CR3 ~0.55), so 2026–27 fit-out deliveries are contractually spoken for before the venture can even bid","refs":["profile:Stage","ledger:cust.operator-large","ledger:shape.cr3"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"obtainableFactor's 3% mid-band is anchored to generic SOM benchmarks, not to the share of the flow that is actually orderable in the window; the pre-let lockout compresses reachable Year-1 demand to replacement and spot buys, well below the band's low end","refs":["ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"3% looks humble — it sits in the middle of every published Year-1 SOM benchmark, so it reads as conservative. The surface appearance of 'we only need a tiny sliver' hides that share of a market is not share of the orderable flow: when 83% of near-term capacity is pre-let, most of the sliver was awarded before launch day.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Two or more CE data-center fit-out tenders with 2026 delivery dates where the rack-PDU line item is still open to new vendors at time of bid (i.e., not pre-committed under a framework), and the venture is admitted to bid.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"The 83% pre-let figure and framework-based procurement in large-operator channels are documented; the counterweight is that replacement/retrofit demand in the 56% non-hyperscale base is genuinely spot-purchased, so full lockout is unlikely but material compression is probable.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"set","value":0.008,"note":"Year-1 orderable flow limited to replacement, change orders, and un-spec'd white-space — below the modeled 1–5% band for reasons the band does not contemplate"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Share of CE fit-out RFQs where rack-PDU vendor is open vs pre-specified","where":"TED (EU tender portal) and operator supplier portals (Equinix, NTT, Data4 RFQ postings)","threshold":"<20% of 2026-delivery RFQs with open PDU line items","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Pre-let ratio of the FLAP-D and CE pipeline","where":"JLL EMEA data centre report (semi-annual)","threshold":"Pre-let staying ≥80% through 2026","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Incumbent backlog commentary on rack power","where":"Vertiv and Schneider quarterly earnings calls","threshold":"Backlog coverage extending beyond 12 months","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Run structured interviews with 3 CE colo/hyperscale procurement leads specifically on WHEN in the build cycle PDU vendors are locked and what share of spend is spot-purchased","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Converts the 1–5% generic benchmark into a flow-based, CE-specific obtainable model"},{"action":"Re-aim the Year-1 motion at replacement/retrofit and white-space expansion in operating colo halls, where PDU purchasing is not gated by pre-let frameworks","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Sells into the only genuinely open segment of the Year-1 flow"}]},{"id":"grid-queue-construction-pause","title":"Frankfurt's 24–36-month grid queue and Amsterdam's 350 MVA cap turn the venture's 12-month window into a delivery trough","narrative":"78% of the modeled TAM sits in Germany and the Netherlands — precisely the two geographies where capacity additions are grid-gated. Frankfurt (60–75% of German capacity) faces 24–36-month grid-connection waits; Amsterdam is at 852 MW with a draft zoning plan capping new connections at 350 MVA until 2030; EMEA-wide, capacity under construction has plateaued at ~2.5 GW despite a 15 GW pipeline. PDU orders land at fit-out milestones, which are gated by energization dates, not by the 9% trend CAGR. If 2026 energizations slip right — and the queue data says many will — Year-1 orders slip with them, regardless of what the market does by 2030.","category":"exogenous","targetNodes":["obtainableFactor"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Frankfurt carries ~745 MW of IT load with grid connections facing 24–36-month waits; Amsterdam's draft umbrella zoning plan caps new connection capacity at 350 MVA until 2030; EMEA under-construction capacity has plateaued at ~2.5 GW on power and permitting constraints","refs":["digest:geo-DE#c20","digest:geo-NL#c8","digest:geo-NL#c3"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Germany (0.50) and the Netherlands (0.28) are 78% of the geographic weight, and Frankfurt alone is 60–75% of the German base — so a grid-driven slip in these two hubs is a slip in most of the venture's addressable fit-out calendar","refs":["ledger:geo.DE","ledger:geo.NL","digest:geo-DE#c1","digest:geo-DE#c4"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The 9% CAGR is a 2030 trend, not a 2026 delivery schedule; YAM is a first-12-months number, so the slip compiles to obtainableFactor (pace), not to the trend node — Year-1 orderable fit-outs shrink even while the long-run TAM is intact","refs":["ledger:shape.cagr","ledger:obtainableFactor","profile:Stage"]}],"whyMissable":"Grid congestion is a known headline, so it appears priced in — but the model prices it nowhere: shape.cagr is a smooth 9% and obtainableFactor is a generic benchmark. The surface appearance of 'strong secular demand' hides that demand in the window is lumpy and gated: pre-let capacity absorbed faster than it can be replaced means orders bunch AFTER energizations, which are exactly what is slipping.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"H1-2026 evidence that Frankfurt and Amsterdam energizations are converting on schedule — e.g., JLL/C&W showing under-construction MW moving to live at or above the prior year's conversion rate in both hubs.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"Queue lengths and the 2.5 GW construction plateau are documented in current reporting; offsetting this, some 2026 deliveries already hold secured connections (e.g., Pure DC's 100 MVA private substation in Amsterdam), so a partial rather than total slip is the modal outcome.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"obtainableFactor","op":"scale","value":0.5,"note":"Roughly half of window-period fit-out milestones in DE/NL slip beyond the 12-month YAM horizon"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Grid-connection queue length and new-connection moratoria in the Frankfurt region","where":"TenneT/Amprion grid development and connection-request disclosures; Hessen regional planning dockets","threshold":"Queue quotes lengthening beyond 36 months or a formal connection pause","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Under-construction → live conversion rate in Amsterdam and Frankfurt","where":"JLL German data center market updates; Cushman & Wakefield EMEA data centre update (H1 2026)","threshold":"Live-capacity additions <15% YoY in either hub","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Finalization of Amsterdam's umbrella zoning plan connection cap","where":"Gemeente Amsterdam zoning consultation docket; DC Byte Amsterdam market page","threshold":"350 MVA cap adopted as drafted","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Build a named-project delivery calendar for the 2026–27 DE/NL fit-out pipeline (project, MW, energization date, GC/electrical contractor) and re-derive Year-1 obtainable from it","type":"information","voiNodeId":"obtainableFactor","note":"Replaces the generic 1–5% band with an energization-gated pipeline model"},{"action":"Add a second sales beachhead in a non-grid-gated CE market (Warsaw, Vienna) to hedge the DE/NL delivery trough","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Poland's additions are growing fastest and are not FLAP-D-queue-bound"},{"action":"Offer stock-and-ship replacement SKUs with short lead times to capture demand that decouples from new-build energization","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Replacement demand in live halls is the segment least exposed to grid gates"}]},{"id":"stock-flow-geo-misprice","title":"The geo split is a museum exhibit: NL's 0.28 is installed stock, but Year-1 revenue lives in the flow — which the Dutch hyperscale ban has already redirected","narrative":"Every geo ratio is derived from a 2025 installed-colocation snapshot. But a pre-launch entrant sells into new fit-outs and expansions — the flow — and the flow map looks nothing like the stock map. Since early 2024 the Netherlands bans new hyperscale data centres outside two municipalities, Amsterdam's connections are capped, and even the DC Byte commentary notes development activity has structurally slowed with 'other markets offering strong alternatives.' Meanwhile investment is shifting to regional hubs and eastward. Weighting Year-1 sales coverage, certification sequencing, and channel hires by a 0.28 NL share systematically points the venture at the geography where the least new PDU-consuming capacity will be commissioned during its window.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["geo.NL","geo.PL","geo.other"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"An amended decree bans new hyperscale data centres (70+ MW) in the Netherlands outside Het Hogeland and Hollands Kroon since early 2024, and Amsterdam's draft zoning plan caps new connections at 350 MVA until 2030 — the NL new-build flow is administratively foreclosed, not merely slow","refs":["digest:geo-NL#c7","digest:geo-NL#c8"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Cushman & Wakefield reports Amsterdam at 852 MW with 'less room for further expansion, while investment is more quickly shifting to other European hubs and to regional locations'; DC Byte notes post-moratorium development runs at a reduced rate — so CE fit-out flow reallocates toward Germany, Poland and the CEE tail while NL's installed stock keeps its 28% weight in the model","refs":["digest:geo-NL#c0","digest:geo-NL#c16","ledger:geo.NL"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"For a first-12-months entrant, PDU demand is proportional to commissioned/expanded capacity in the window, not to standing stock; geo.NL's 0.28 overstates the NL slice of orderable demand while geo.PL (0.07) and geo.other (0.03) — where the derivations already flag divergent, faster-growing scopes — understate theirs","refs":["ledger:geo.PL","ledger:geo.other","profile:Stage"]}],"whyMissable":"The NL node looks like the model's safest: triangulated, two sources agreeing within 3%. That agreement is precisely the trap — both sources measure the same stock quantity, and stock is the wrong quantity for a Year-1 revenue model. A number can be perfectly sourced and still be the answer to a different question.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"DDA's 2026 State of the Dutch Data Centers (or Statista's 2026 update) showing NL colocation MW additions at or above 28% of total CE-7 additions for the year.","likelihood":{"value":0.55,"rationale":"The hyperscale ban, the Amsterdam connection cap, and C&W's explicit 'investment shifting' statement are all in force today; the uncertainty is magnitude (retrofit and the two exempt municipalities keep some NL flow alive), not direction.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"geo.NL","op":"set","value":0.18,"note":"NL flow share well below its stock share given the ban and connection cap"},{"nodeId":"geo.PL","op":"set","value":0.11,"note":"Poland absorbs a larger share of window-period additions"},{"nodeId":"geo.other","op":"set","value":0.05,"note":"CEE tail (esp. Romania) takes redirected flow; net effect is a modest TAM haircut plus a mix shift"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"NL colocation capacity additions vs CE-7 additions","where":"Dutch Data Center Association annual State of the Dutch Data Centers report","threshold":"NL additions <15% of CE-7 additions in 2026","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Exemption-zone activity (Eemshaven, Agriport) vs rest-of-NL permitting","where":"Het Hogeland / Hollands Kroon municipal permit registers; DC Byte Amsterdam market updates","threshold":"No new non-exempt NL hyperscale permits granted in 2026","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Poland/CEE commissioning announcements","where":"PMR Poland data-center market updates; Atman/Data4/Vantage Warsaw press releases","threshold":"≥100 MW of new CE-east commissionings announced for 2026–27","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Rebuild the geography weights on a 2026–27 additions basis (announced commissioning MW per country) instead of installed stock, and re-run the funnel","type":"information","voiNodeId":"geo.NL","note":"One analyst-week using DC Byte/C&W pipeline data; directly re-prices the NL/PL/CEE mix"},{"action":"Sequence go-to-market coverage Frankfurt-first, Warsaw-second, and treat NL as a retrofit-only territory in Year 1","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Aligns scarce launch resources with where PDUs will actually be ordered in the window"}]},{"id":"statista-stage-blend-tambase","title":"If Statista's country MW blends pipeline stages, the CE apportionment inside tamBase is counting halls that don't exist yet","narrative":"tamBase's only CE-specific hop is 'CE-7 = 39.8% of Europe colo MW (Statista).' But the corpus shows Statista-style country figures may not be operational-only: summed Statista country colo exceeds CBRE's total European capacity, and the same digest shows how easily vendors blend stages — DC Byte's Amsterdam 'supply' is 1,849 MW while operational capacity is 852 MW, a 2.2x gap. If the Statista series leans toward committed/under-construction capacity, the CE share and the country weights are computed on MW that generate zero PDU revenue in 2026 — and with EMEA construction plateaued at 2.5 GW, much of that phantom base stays phantom through the venture's window. This is a different failure than the ±40% band contemplates: the band prices scope-down uncertainty on the global number, not stage-blending in the one CE-specific multiplier.","category":"fact","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Vendor capacity figures demonstrably blend pipeline stages: DC Byte reports Amsterdam 'Total Supply' of 1,849 MW (live + under construction + committed + early stage) against 852 MW operational (C&W) — the same city, a 2.2x gap purely from stage definition","refs":["digest:geo-NL#c6","digest:geo-NL#c0"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"tamBase multiplies the €775M Europe estimate by 'CE-7 = 39.8% of Europe colo MW' from the Statista series, whose scope ('colocation and scale-colocation IT power') no second vendor replicates; if it includes non-operational MW, the euro-revenue-relevant CE base is smaller — and with EMEA under-construction plateaued at ~2.5 GW, blended-in pipeline MW does not convert to PDU-buying halls inside 12 months","refs":["ledger:tamBase","digest:geo-NL#c3"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The 2025/26 revenue-relevant tamBase sits below the curated €320M for a reason orthogonal to the global-number spread the sensitivity band was built from; every downstream node inherits the overstatement proportionally","refs":["ledger:tamBase","digest:tam-global-crosscheck#c0"]}],"whyMissable":"The 240–520 band looks generous, so 'TAM might be smaller' feels handled. But the band's low end was derived from the GLOBAL number's publisher spread; nobody stress-tested the single CE-specific hop — the Statista MW apportionment — for stage-blending, because two capacity 'methods' appeared to triangulate it. Both methods share the same untested scope definition.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Statista methodology documentation (or direct reconciliation) confirming the 'colocation and scale-colocation IT power' series counts operational MW only, matching C&W/CBRE operational figures within ~10% for DE and NL.","likelihood":{"value":0.3,"rationale":"The Amsterdam 852 vs 951 MW and Germany 1,737 vs multiple divergent totals suggest partial rather than gross blending; the >2x DC Byte-style inflation is possible but the Statista figures sit closer to operational anchors than to full-pipeline ones.","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"set","value":250,"note":"CE apportionment recomputed on operational-only MW; lands near the band's low edge for a cause outside the band's logic"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Per-country operational vs pipeline MW splits for CE-7","where":"Cushman & Wakefield EMEA Data Centre Update (semi-annual, country tables)","threshold":"Statista country figure exceeding C&W operational figure by >20% for DE or NL","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"DC Byte live vs total-supply ratios for Frankfurt, Amsterdam, Warsaw","where":"DC Byte market pages / quarterly market summaries","threshold":"Live share of total supply <55% in the CE hubs","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission the IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report (country tables) to anchor the CE base in directly-scoped revenue rather than MW apportionment","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"Already the ledger's named promotion path; it removes the Statista hop entirely"},{"action":"Rebuild the CE apportionment using one consistent operational-only source (C&W or CBRE country tables) across all seven countries","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"Cheap desk exercise that isolates how much of the 39.8% is stage-blend artifact"}]},{"id":"large-operator-qualification-tunnel","title":"'Large operators first' aims the launch at the one channel that cannot buy inside 12 months","narrative":"The venture's stated first channel is large operators — and the model rewards that choice with the biggest buyer node (0.40, the widest-swing customer share). But that 0.40 is an internal estimate resting on a single misread order-mix statistic, and it says nothing about ACCESS. Large colo and hyperscale operators buy rack power through approved-vendor lists and framework agreements: qualification (CE/TÜV, EN 50600 alignment, firmware security review, DCIM interoperability, pilot pods) routinely runs 12–24 months for a new hardware vendor. For a pre-launch entrant, the large-operator slice is not a smaller number — it is unavailable inside the YAM window. Year-1 reachable demand collapses onto OEM/integrator and distributor channels the model weights at only 0.35 combined and has never validated.","category":"execution","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The go-to-market plan names large operators as the first buyer, and the model assigns them the dominant 0.40 buyer share — a share whose only evidence is one supplier's company-level order mix, with the ledger itself flagging 'internal estimate, no published channel split'","refs":["profile:Stage","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Large operators' near-term deliveries are pre-let (83%) and vendor-committed under frameworks with a semi-consolidated incumbent set (CR3 ~0.55); a new vendor must first clear AVL qualification, which for powered rack hardware exceeds the venture's entire 12-month horizon — so the channel is sequentially blocked, not just competitive","refs":["digest:geo-DE#c21","ledger:shape.cr3"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Within the YAM window the operator-large cell is foreclosed: Year-1 obtainable demand must route through OEM/integrator (0.22) and distributor (0.13) cells — both zero-evidence estimates — shrinking reachable SAM by the excluded share","refs":["ledger:cust.oem","ledger:cust.distributor","ledger:cust.operator-large"]}],"whyMissable":"Choosing the biggest buyer as the first target looks like focus, and the model validates it by making that buyer the largest node. The surface appearance — 'we're going where the money is' — hides a sequencing constraint: the size of a channel and the speed of entry into it are different variables, and the model only encodes the first.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A documented case (or direct confirmation from a CE large operator's procurement team) of a new rack-PDU vendor moving from first contact to purchase order in under 9 months, including qualification and pilot.","likelihood":{"value":0.55,"rationale":"Vendor-qualification timelines of 12+ months for powered rack hardware at large operators are the industry norm (base rate for new-entrant hardware onboarding at enterprise/hyperscale accounts), and nothing in the venture's pre-launch position shortens them; partial exceptions exist via urgent-capacity waivers.","basis":"base-rate"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"exclude","value":null,"note":"Large-operator cell unavailable to the venture within the Year-1 window — foreclosure, not shrinkage"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Stated vendor-qualification requirements and timelines in operator supplier onboarding portals","where":"Equinix, NTT GDC, Data4, Vantage supplier/procurement portals and RFQ documents","threshold":"Mandatory qualification stages summing to >9 months before first PO eligibility","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Venture's own qualification funnel progress with the first two target operators","where":"Internal CRM / operator procurement correspondence, reviewed monthly","threshold":"No pilot-pod agreement signed within 4 months of launch","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Distributor and rack-integrator line-card openings for third-party intelligent PDUs","where":"CE electrical distributor price lists (Rexel, Sonepar) and rack-integrator OEM programs","threshold":"≥1 CE distributor actively onboarding a new PDU brand","updates":"decreases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Interview 3 CE operator procurement heads and 2 distributors specifically on qualification duration, AVL entry criteria, and whether any fast-track (urgent capacity) lane exists","type":"information","voiNodeId":"cust.operator-large","note":"Also the ledger's own promotion path for the entire unsourced buyer-mix block"},{"action":"Enter Year 1 through OEM/rack-integrator white-label supply, where the integrator's existing AVL status carries the product into large-operator halls","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts the qualification tunnel from a blocker into someone else's sunk cost"},{"action":"Start large-operator qualification processes in parallel at launch so the channel opens in Year 2, and model Year-1 revenue exclusively on indirect channels","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Decouples the pipeline-building activity from the Year-1 revenue plan"}]}]}
